items.myth: Poetry is just a wrapper around pip freeze.
items.reality: It uses a dependency solver to compute a consistent set and records it with hashes in poetry.lock — far beyond freezing whatever happens to be installed.
items.example: `pip freeze` writes what's currently installed (including stale/conflicting versions). `poetry lock` runs a SAT-like solver against your manifest's *constraints* and emits a coherent, hash-verified resolution.

items.myth: You still have to manage a venv manually with Poetry.
items.reality: Poetry creates and manages a virtual environment for the project automatically; poetry run / poetry shell use it.
items.example: First `poetry install` creates `~/.cache/pypoetry/virtualenvs/myproj-XXXX-py3.11/`. `poetry run pytest` runs inside it. Configure `poetry config virtualenvs.in-project true` if you'd rather have `.venv/` in the repo.

items.reality: pyproject.toml is a standard Python config file (PEP 518/621); Poetry uses it but so do other tools like pip, build, and ruff.
items.example: The same `pyproject.toml` can hold `[tool.poetry.*]`, `[tool.ruff]`, `[tool.mypy]`, `[tool.pytest.ini_options]` — Poetry owns its tables; other tools own theirs. One file, many consumers.
